title: Mission 01 — Lexington to Louisville
summary: A daytime VFR cross-country from KLEX to KLOU. Benign weather, light winds, and a low-pressure radio workload make this the tutorial mission.

weather.summary: Excellent VFR with scattered clouds and manageable southwest winds.

notams: KLOU taxiway Alpha closed between A3 and A5.

personalMinimums: No ceilings below 3000 ft for solo XC

events.title: Go / No-Go
events.prompt: Weather is solid VFR and the aircraft is within limits. What is the best ADM decision?

options.label: Launch as planned with a standard VFR briefing.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Conditions are well within personal minimums and aircraft capability.

options.label: Delay for several hours even though no risk factor requires it.

consequence.feedback: Conservative, but not supported by the current risk picture.

options.label: Cancel because any cross-country introduces risk.

consequence.feedback: Risk exists, but ADM weighs actual hazards rather than avoiding all exposure.

events.title: Weight & Balance
events.prompt: With one passenger, 56 gallons, and 20 lb of baggage, what should you conclude?

options.label: The airplane is under max gross and acceptable for departure.

consequence.feedback: Correct. The loading is within the aircraft's 3,100 lb max gross for this mission.

options.label: Dump fuel to 35 gallons because the airplane is overweight.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. The current loading is already within limits.

options.label: Skip the calculation because the airplane feels light enough.

consequence.feedback: Poor ADM. Weight and balance must be computed, not guessed.

events.title: Fuel Planning
events.prompt: The trip is about 58 NM. What fuel plan best matches good preflight planning?

options.label: Carry 56 gallons, giving ample endurance and reserve.

consequence.feedback: Correct. This provides more than enough fuel while staying efficient.

options.label: Launch with 12 gallons because the flight is short.

consequence.feedback: Unsafe. A short flight still requires legal reserve and contingency planning.

options.label: Top off to 87 gallons even if it reduces performance margin without need.

consequence.feedback: Legal, but not ideal. More fuel is not automatically better if it adds unnecessary weight.

events.title: Departure Frequency
events.prompt: You're handed off after departure. Which response is best?

consequence.feedback: Acceptable but incomplete. Altitude helps ATC confirm your status.

options.label: Hey approach, this is the Skylane from Lexington heading over your way.

consequence.feedback: Nonstandard phraseology increases workload and confusion.

events.title: Class C Transition
events.prompt: Approach offers a transition near the Louisville Class C shelf. What's the best action?

options.label: Read back the clearance and continue while monitoring the assigned frequency.

consequence.feedback: Correct. You accepted the service and remain compliant inside controlled airspace.

options.label: Continue through the shelf and self-announce if needed.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. You need two-way communication and any required clearance for the offered transition.

options.label: Decline and divert south to stay entirely clear of the shelf.

consequence.feedback: Conservative and safe, but less efficient than accepting the valid ATC clearance.

events.title: Fuel Cross-Check
events.prompt: You're established on course with plenty of fuel. What in-flight habit best supports ADM?

options.label: Cross-check fuel burn against time and verify reserves remain healthy.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Good pilots continuously verify assumptions during flight.

options.label: Ignore the gauges because the preflight plan already covered fuel.

consequence.feedback: Weak ADM. Fuel status must be monitored in flight, not assumed.

options.label: Aggressively lean for max economy without checking engine indications.

consequence.feedback: Poor systems management. Leaning decisions should be tied to engine indications and POH guidance.
